content.assessment: This proposal most directly follows the Finnish structural template: delaying first tracking, converting into a unified comprehensive phase, and explicitly recognizing (via the bundling requirement) that Finland's reform worked because delay was combined with ability-grouping abolition and curriculum/teacher-training change, not delay in isolation. This aligns closely with the lens's core criteria of tracking age and comprehensive provision. However, the lens also supplies a critical caution: Finland's own comprehensive system has experienced significant later erosion, including a 64-point PISA mathematics decline (2003-2022) with growing SES achievement gaps, and internally-generated informal sorting via 'emphasized classes' since the 1990s, plus broader school-choice-driven segregation. This means structural delay is necessary but historically insufficient for durable equity; political durability and continued vigilance against informal substitution are required, and Hungary's demographic/institutional context differs from Finland's, limiting confidence in direct transferability.
opportunities: Anti-sorting catchment rules bundled with delay could pre-emptively address the informal-selection substitution risk that undermined Finland's own comprehensive ideal over time (F-live-finnish_reform-08, F-live-finnish_reform-09)
strengths: Explicitly incorporates the bundling lesson from Finland's reform (curriculum and choice-regulation alongside delay), directly matching the mechanism Finland found necessary (F-live-finnish_reform-06)
Targets both tracking age and comprehensive provision simultaneously, the two criteria most central to this lens
threats: Finland's experience of 'emphasized classes' and choice-driven segregation demonstrates that formal comprehensive structure alone is not self-enforcing; without continuous institutional vigilance, informal selection mechanisms are likely to re-emerge inside the Hungarian unified phase as well (F-live-finnish_reform-08, F-live-finnish_reform-09)

Political durability is a real risk: Finland's egalitarian design required sustained political commitment over decades, and even that has eroded amid a 64-point score decline, suggesting a Hungarian reform without comparable sustained political consensus faces even greater durability risk (F-live-finnish_reform-07)
weaknesses: Even a well-bundled comprehensive reform did not prevent later performance decline and growing inequality in Finland, indicating the proposal's theory of change may achieve initial equity gains without guaranteeing durability (F-live-finnish_reform-07)
Contextual differences between Finland and Hungary (noted as a lens limitation) constrain confidence that the same institutional design will produce similar outcomes
